bins.name: Oppose Federal Jurisdiction Expansion
bins.desc: The KCC consistently argues that an unprecedented federal jurisdictional grab is not the right response to large load growth. It contends existing state-federal boundaries are workable, that the ANOPR fails to analyze the full scope of jurisdictional issues, and that certain reform principles could allow large loads to bypass state-franchised utilities in non-restructured states.
quotes: the ANOPR proposes a process that does not address the heart of the issues
the KCC takes issue with the conclusion that an unprecedented expansion of federal jurisdiction is necessary to address these important issues
the appropriate response involves adherence to the well-established divide between state and federal jurisdiction and a commitment to cooperative federalism
This principle suggests that large load customers have a right to directly interconnect to the FERC-regulated grid that trumps state laws regarding retail choice and certificated service territories.

FERC should expressly reject any proposals that allow large load customers to bypass retail utilities in non-restructured states in order to purchase power directly from a wholesale market or an independent generator.

the ANOPR provides no details on implementation. Given the magnitude of the proposed expansion of federal jurisdiction, FERC should take care to fully identify and analyze the relevant issues and avoid relying on unsupported statements about the ANOPR's impact on state jurisdiction

bins.name: Hybrid Co-location and Bypass Risk
bins.desc: The KCC flags that ANOPR reform principle 9, which gives large load customers an option to build similar to generator interconnection customers, could be read to override state franchise rights and certificated service territories, including by allowing third-party generators to co-locate with large loads in areas served by exclusive franchisees.
quotes: This principle suggests that large load customers have a right to directly interconnect to the FERC-regulated grid that trumps state laws regarding retail choice and certificated service territories.

bins.name: Network Upgrade Cost Allocation
bins.desc: The KCC agrees large loads should pay upgrade costs assigned through interconnection studies, but argues the bigger problem is cost allocation for regional transmission expansion plans that states cannot fully address. It supports the goal of protecting traditional load from cost shifts but says the ANOPR's focus on interconnection-study upgrades is too narrow.
quotes: The minimum bill calculation includes payment of a reasonable allocation of all transmission costs incurred by the retail utility to serve retail customers, including network upgrades constructed as a result of interconnection studies or other regional transmission expansion plans.

the focus on cost allocation within interconnection studies is misplaced. The larger issue is the cost increases and potential cost shifts associated with regional transmission expansion plans, which states do not have authority to fully address.
there is currently no mechanism under federal statutes or regulations that require projected loads to commit to pay for the regional projects ahead of building those projects

bins.name: Pre-commitment to Regional Costs
bins.desc: The KCC identifies a gap in federal law: unlike its own LLPS tariff, nothing currently requires large loads to commit to paying for regional transmission projects before those projects are built. It views this as a necessary federal reform that can be addressed within the existing jurisdictional structure.
quotes: there is currently no mechanism under federal statutes or regulations that require projected loads to commit to pay for the regional projects ahead of building those projects

bins.name: Oppose April 2026 Action Deadline
bins.desc: The KCC urges FERC to reject the April 30, 2026 deadline for final action, arguing the unprecedented nature of the proposed jurisdictional expansion and the absence of any specific reform proposals make the timeline unreasonable and likely to produce poorly vetted rules.
quotes: the KCC is concerned that any effort by FERC expand its jurisdiction by April 30, 2026 as the ANOPR proposes does not allow for the full scope of issues to be identified and addressed

bins.name: Traditional Load Cost Protection
bins.desc: The KCC strongly supports protecting existing customers from cost shifts caused by large load interconnection. It points to Kansas's LLPS tariff as a model, but argues federal cost allocation reforms tied to regional transmission planning are also needed because state tools cannot reach wholesale regional upgrade costs.
quotes: the KCC approved a unanimous settlement agreement that will establish a new Large Load Power Service ("LLPS") rate plan that is designed to serve electric customers that reasonably expect to need a peak load of at least 75 MW

The minimum bill calculation includes payment of a reasonable allocation of all transmission costs incurred by the retail utility to serve retail customers, including network upgrades constructed as a result of interconnection studies or other regional transmission expansion plans.

the focus on cost allocation within interconnection studies is misplaced. The larger issue is the cost increases and potential cost shifts associated with regional transmission expansion plans, which states do not have authority to fully address.
there is currently no mechanism under federal statutes or regulations that require projected loads to commit to pay for the regional projects ahead of building those projects

the KCC notes the harm that traditional loads would be subjected to if the large loads that formed the basis for the transmission plans do not materialize, leaving only existing customers to pay for generational expansions of the transmission grid

bins.name: Regional Stakeholder Study Process
bins.desc: The KCC favors RTO-level stakeholder processes to develop targeted tariff reforms rather than top-down FERC rulemaking. It argues regional stakeholders are best placed to craft narrow wholesale reforms that respect jurisdictional boundaries while addressing the underlying problems.
quotes: the focus on cost allocation within interconnection studies is misplaced. The larger issue is the cost increases and potential cost shifts associated with regional transmission expansion plans, which states do not have authority to fully address.
stakeholders at the RTO level are best positioned to address this issue by crafting the narrow reforms referred to in Section II.A.1 and presenting those reforms to FERC

regional stakeholders are best positioned to develop reforms that meet the dual objectives of respecting jurisdictional boundaries and effectively addressing the underlying issues that are at the heart of this inquiry

bins.name: SPP Region Context
bins.desc: The KCC draws on SPP-specific experience throughout its comments, including Kansas's LLPS tariff, SPP's proactive regional transmission expansion planning, and the risk that existing SPP customers could be left holding costs for billions of dollars in transmission projects if large loads do not materialize.
quotes: the KCC approved a unanimous settlement agreement that will establish a new Large Load Power Service ("LLPS") rate plan that is designed to serve electric customers that reasonably expect to need a peak load of at least 75 MW

The minimum bill calculation includes payment of a reasonable allocation of all transmission costs incurred by the retail utility to serve retail customers, including network upgrades constructed as a result of interconnection studies or other regional transmission expansion plans.

the KCC notes the harm that traditional loads would be subjected to if the large loads that formed the basis for the transmission plans do not materialize, leaving only existing customers to pay for generational expansions of the transmission grid
stakeholders at the RTO level are best positioned to address this issue by crafting the narrow reforms referred to in Section II.A.1 and presenting those reforms to FERC

regional stakeholders are best positioned to develop reforms that meet the dual objectives of respecting jurisdictional boundaries and effectively addressing the underlying issues that are at the heart of this inquiry

bins.name: ANOPR Lacks Implementation Detail
bins.desc: The KCC criticizes the ANOPR for containing no concrete reform proposals while claiming broad jurisdictional reach, and for relying on unsupported statements about its impact on state authority. It argues FERC cannot evaluate the ANOPR's state-jurisdiction assurances until specific reforms are proposed and analyzed.
quotes: the ANOPR provides no details on implementation. Given the magnitude of the proposed expansion of federal jurisdiction, FERC should take care to fully identify and analyze the relevant issues and avoid relying on unsupported statements about the ANOPR's impact on state jurisdiction
the KCC is concerned that any effort by FERC expand its jurisdiction by April 30, 2026 as the ANOPR proposes does not allow for the full scope of issues to be identified and addressed

bins.name: Cooperative Federalism Approach
bins.desc: The KCC frames cooperative federalism as the correct organizing principle: states address generation and local distribution costs through mechanisms like the LLPS, while RTOs develop narrow wholesale transmission reforms and bring them to FERC. This approach is presented as both legally sound and better at producing workable policy.
quotes: the appropriate response involves adherence to the well-established divide between state and federal jurisdiction and a commitment to cooperative federalism
stakeholders at the RTO level are best positioned to address this issue by crafting the narrow reforms referred to in Section II.A.1 and presenting those reforms to FERC

regional stakeholders are best positioned to develop reforms that meet the dual objectives of respecting jurisdictional boundaries and effectively addressing the underlying issues that are at the heart of this inquiry
